note: In early 2024, a malicious backdoor was discovered embedded in XZ Utils, a widely used compression library in Linux distributions, designed to enable unauthorized remote code execution via OpenSSH using an Ed448 private key. The perpetrator, operating under the pseudonym Jia Tan, spent over two years building trust as a contributor before inserting the exploit, which received a maximum CVSS score of 10.0. The backdoor was discovered by Microsoft engineer Andres Freund, who noticed anomalous SSH performance; fortunately, affected versions had not yet reached most production systems at the time of disclosure. The incident prompted broad discussion about the fragility of critical open-source infrastructure and the risks posed by long-running social engineering campaigns targeting software maintainers.
title: XZ Utils backdoor - Wikipedia

note: An arXiv research paper presenting an evidence-driven analysis of why threat information sharing for industrial control systems remains ineffective. Drawing on three major ICS attacks and a review of 196 procedure examples across 22 malware families, it identifies limitations including incomplete support in the STIX sharing standard, reliance on proprietary undocumented protocols, and insufficient technical detail in threat and vulnerability reports. It concludes that expanding STIX to accommodate ICS-specific artifacts is essential for usable threat intelligence sharing.
title: An Evidence-Driven Analysis of Threat Information Sharing ... - arXiv

note: A Barnes & Noble listing for Advanced Criminal Investigations and Intelligence Operations by Robert J. Girod. The book examines tradecraft, defined as the methods, practices, and techniques used in espionage and clandestine investigations. It surveys how these techniques appear across contexts, from government covert work to criminal practitioners, organized around tradecraft methods, practices, tactics, and techniques relevant to both criminal investigation and intelligence operations.
title: Advanced Criminal Investigations and Intelligence Operations: Tradecraft Methods, Practices, Tactics, and Techniques|Paperback

note: The Wikipedia article on financial intelligence (FININT), defined as gathering information about the financial affairs of entities of interest to understand their nature, capabilities, and intentions. It outlines two core components: collection, in which Financial Intelligence Units gather transaction data and suspicious activity reports from banks, and analysis, in which specialists use data mining to detect tax evasion, money laundering, and terrorist financing. It notes the role of US Treasury bodies and international data-sharing, and discusses challenges posed by informal value transfer systems that operate outside regulated banking.
title: Financial intelligence - Wikipedia

note: A Wikipedia article on financial intelligence (FININT), the gathering and analysis of information about financial transactions to detect illicit activity such as money laundering, tax evasion, and terrorist financing. It distinguishes two core functions: collection of raw transaction data and suspicious activity reports by government financial intelligence units, and analysis using data-matching techniques to identify suspicious patterns. The article describes the U.S. institutional structure under the Treasury Department, including FinCEN and the Office of Foreign Assets Control.
title: Financial intelligence - Wikipedia

note: The Citizendium 'Financial intelligence' article was never written; the page is empty. Use an authoritative alternative source for financial intelligence definitions.
title: Financial intelligence

note: A 2025 article by Kevin Hk Chow in Modern Economy titled "Financial Intelligence: A Crucial Pillar of National Security." It argues that Financial Intelligence (FININT) serves as a critical interface between economic systems and national security, used to detect and disrupt illicit financial networks tied to money laundering, terrorism financing, and transnational crime. The paper frames FININT not merely as a set of tools but as a dynamic, adaptive network of interdependent nodes, including financial institutions, intelligence agencies, and regulatory bodies, that collectively build systemic resilience against financial threats to national stability.
title: Financial Intelligence: A Crucial Pillar of National Security

note: Joint Publication 3-53, the US Department of Defense joint doctrine for psychological operations. It establishes organizational structure, command relationships, planning processes, and approval authorities for integrating psychological operations into joint military campaigns and peacetime activities. The document frames these operations as a component of information operations and addresses intelligence integration, logistics, multinational coordination, and theater security cooperation across command levels.
title: [PDF] JP 3-53, "Doctrine for Joint Psychological Operations"

note: Joint Publication 3-53, Doctrine for Joint Psychological Operations (Joint Chiefs of Staff, 10 July 1996). This US military doctrine document establishes the doctrinal basis for planning and conducting psychological operations in support of joint military operations. It identifies the policy and authority sources, the responsibilities of Defense and other government agencies, basic principles and objectives, capabilities, and the planning, logistics, intelligence, and coordination considerations required to integrate such operations into joint and interagency efforts.
title: [PDF] JP 3-53 Doctrine for Joint Psychological Operations

note: It sets forth joint doctrine to govern the activities and performance of the Armed Forces of the Uni...
title: [PDF] Military Information Support Operations

note: A U.S. military field manual, FM 3-05.302, providing doctrine for tactical psychological operations at the unit level. The roughly 255-page document covers the organization of these units, command relationships when supporting joint forces, a multi-phase product development and assessment process, and how intelligence supports such operations. It serves as a comprehensive training and reference document for personnel involved in tactical influence operations.
title: Tactical Psychological Operations Manual | PDF | Computers - Scribd

note: ... Psychological Operations (PSYOP) doctrine presented in the higher-level publication, FM 3-05.30,...
title: [PDF] FM 3-05.301 Psychological Operations Process Tactics, Techniques ...

note: A U.S. Department of State Bureau of Public Affairs report from October 1981, released under FOIA. It catalogs Soviet 'active measures' — the Soviet term for covert influence operations — including written disinformation, control of foreign media, use of front organizations, clandestine broadcasting, blackmail, and political influence operations. Specific cases include Soviet forgeries related to the 1979 Grand Mosque seizure and NATO theater nuclear force debates.
title: SOVIET 'ACTIVE MEASURES' FORGERY, DISINFORMATION, POLITICAL OPERATIONS

note: An article by Dennis Kux published in Parameters: Journal of the US Army War College (1985) and preserved in the CIA CREST archive. It defines and distinguishes Soviet 'disinformation' and 'active measures,' situating them within a spectrum of white, gray, and black foreign influence operations. Kux examines KGB front groups, agents of influence, forgeries, and the broader strategic goal of tarnishing Western governments while advancing Soviet foreign policy.
title: SOVIET ACTIVE MEASURES AND DISINFORMATION: OVERVIEW AND ASSESSMENT

note: A CIA CREST record (October 1981, released March 2007) comprising a routing and record sheet from the PCS/PGLO office dated February 8, 1982, attached to the same Department of State Special Report No. 88 on Soviet active measures. The document is catalogued as an open-source CREST entry and captures the internal CIA circulation of the State Department's public report on Soviet forgery and disinformation operations.
title: "SOVIET ACTIVE MEASURES: FORGERY, DISINFORMATION, POLITICAL OPERATIONS"

note: A declassified-in-part 1986 CIA speech text on Soviet active measures, prepared by the Foreign Activities Branch, Third World Activities Division, Office of Soviet Analysis. It explains the KGB's use of forgeries, disinformation, and front organizations to covertly influence foreign governments and publics, illustrating with the forged 1984 Olympic KKK leaflet operation designed to discourage participation in the Los Angeles Games.
title: SOVIET ACTIVE MEASURES AND DISINFORMATION

note: A March 2019 CREST (Centre for Research and Evidence on Security Threats) report titled "Russia and Disinformation: Maskirovka," prepared by scholars of Russian foreign and security policy using open-source and Russian-language primary material. It examines the historical background of Soviet and Russian disinformation, including the concept of active measures, and how disinformation is woven into strategic narratives and diplomacy. The report discusses the aims, targets, and dissemination methods of such activities and the difficulty of tracing their influence on target audiences.
title: INTRODUCTION

note: Fletcher Schoen and Christopher J. Lamb, 'Deception, Disinformation, and Strategic Communications: How One Interagency Group Made a Major Difference,' INSS Strategic Perspectives No. 11 (NDU Press, June 2012). The study examines the U.S. Active Measures Working Group, a small interagency committee formed in the 1980s to expose and counter Soviet disinformation, and analyzes why it succeeded where most such groups fail.
title: Deception, Disinformation, and Strategic Communications

note: Fletcher Schoen and Christopher J. Lamb, 'Deception, Disinformation, and Strategic Communications: How One Interagency Group Made a Major Difference,' INSS Strategic Perspectives No. 11 (NDU Press, June 2012). The study traces the Active Measures Working Group from its founding through its decline, arguing that senior political support, a focused unclassified mission, and shared expertise enabled the group to raise the cost of Soviet disinformation and helped convince Gorbachev to abandon key campaigns.
title: Deception, Disinformation, and Strategic Communications: How One Interagency Group Made a Major Difference

note: This policy paper examines the emergence of cognitive security as a distinct and critical strategic ...
title: (PDF) COGNITIVE SECURITY IN THE AGE OF AI - Academia.edu

note: A position paper (Stanford authors, ICLR 2026 workshop) arguing that AI research and development should prioritize cognitive security, defined as protecting human cognitive processes from hazardous influence. It notes that generative AI systems increasingly designed to influence beliefs and behavior raise acute governance concerns, while research on these effects remains fragmented. The paper proposes treating influence over human cognition as a security problem, outlining an attack-and-defense research cycle and metrics to measure belief change, durability, dependence, and awareness of AI influence.
title: [PDF] AI DEVELOPMENT SHOULD PRIORITIZE COGNITIVE SECURITY

note: This Mitnick Security guide traces the history and evolution of social engineering from antiquity through modern cyberattacks. It surveys major attack categories including phishing and its variants, pretexting, baiting, tailgating, and quid pro quo schemes, and explains the psychological influence principles such as reciprocity, scarcity, authority, and consensus that attackers exploit. The resource includes case studies and emphasizes awareness training, verification practices, and multi-factor authentication as defenses.
title: The History of Social Engineering - Mitnick Security Consulting

note: The full text of "Social Engineering: The Science of Human Hacking" (2nd edition, 2018) by Christopher Hadnagy, published by John Wiley & Sons. The book examines the human element of security, analyzing how manipulation and persuasion techniques can be used to influence people, and how individuals and organizations can recognize and defend against such tactics. It is written as a professional reference on understanding and countering human-targeted security threats.
title: [PDF] Social Engineering: T he Science of Human Hacking - The Swiss Bay

note: A PDF of the book Social Engineering: The Art of Human Hacking by Christopher Hadnagy, a foundational security text on how attackers exploit human psychology rather than technical flaws to obtain information or access. The work surveys manipulation tactics and the human factors that make organizations vulnerable, and frames this knowledge defensively to help security professionals recognize and resist such attacks. The document is a several-hundred-page complete copy of the book.
title: [PDF] Social Engineering: The Art of Human Hacking

note: This survey examines how large language model-based agents can be weaponized as autonomous cyberattack tools, analyzing their architecture across five core components: models, perception, memory, reasoning and planning, and tools. The authors document eight attack categories and map them across static infrastructure, mobile networks, and infrastructure-free systems such as blockchain and the metaverse. A central finding is that LLM-based agents dramatically reduce the expertise and resources required to execute multi-stage intrusions, with demonstrated frameworks achieving initial access within minutes at minimal cost. Existing defensive mechanisms are described as poorly equipped to counter continuously operating, dynamically adapting automated adversaries.
title: A Survey on Large Language Model-based Agents in Autonomous ...

note: The military's use or misuse of information in psychological or deception operations, under the guis...
title: [PDF] Information Warfare: An Air Force Policy for the Role of Public Affairs

note: A 2006 paper by William Hutchinson published in the journal Informing Science. It examines the history of information warfare and the increasingly central role of deception within it, tracing the concept from its late-1980s origins as a technology-oriented military tactic for command-and-control dominance to a broader recognition of information itself as both weapon and target. The paper, focused largely on Western liberal democracies and especially the United States, argues that managing and manipulating information became an essential function of official government communication.
title: [PDF] Information Warfare and Deception

note: A topic page from the Journal of Information Warfare collecting scholarly articles on military deception. Featured pieces argue that information operations officers should be conceptualized primarily as deception officers, and one quantitative reassessment reports that deception is employed in roughly 30 to 53 percent of military operations. Other articles examine how Russian-Soviet strategic culture shaped deception across historical conflicts and analyze ancient campaigns against modern principles of military deception. The journal positions itself as a venue for research on information warfare, information operations, and cyber crime.
title: Military Deception | Journal of Information Warfare

note: An essay published in Small Wars Journal (June 2025) by a U.S. Army Civil Affairs officer arguing that military deception and information operations have become central to modern conflict. It draws on the Second Nagorno-Karabakh War and the Russo-Ukrainian War, noting how reliance on integrated sensor systems and data-driven decision-making creates exploitable vulnerabilities, and citing Ukraine's 2022 effort to misdirect Russian expectations toward Kherson while striking Kharkiv. The author recommends that U.S. services better integrate and standardize information-warfare capabilities across branches.
title: The Utility of Military Deception and Information Operations in ...

note: Sarah Rajtmajer and Daniel Susser, 'Automated Influence and the Challenge of Cognitive Security,' in Hot Topics in the Science of Security (HotSoS '20), April 2020, ACM. The paper argues that AI-powered computational propaganda poses national security threats that existing ethical frameworks do not address, and proposes 'cognitive security' as a productive conceptual lens for evaluating the ethics of automated influence operations and potential defensive responses.
title: Automated influence and the challenge of cognitive security

note: A 2024 bachelor's thesis by Lara Sakarya at Delft University of Technology presenting a systematic literature review of the active inference framework and the free-energy principle as applied to mimicking social human behavior in intelligent agents. It explains active inference as a theory describing behavior that minimizes surprise, and surveys model variants such as deep active inference, multimodal deep belief networks, predictive coding, and probabilistic programming. The review identifies their relevance, methodologies, and limitations, noting challenges in scaling to high-dimensional data, computational demands, and translating simplified models to real-world applications.
title: [PDF] Applications of The Active Inference and The Free-Energy Principle ...

note: An arXiv research paper (March 2026) proposing an integrative modeling framework for human cognitive security, defined as the degree to which people rely on truthful information to make truth-aligned judgments. It synthesizes Bayesian belief-updating with Prospect Theory to model how cognitive resources and emotional valuation shape behavior, and identifies three observable outcomes: veracity discernment, task-oriented actions, and information sharing. The framework accounts for phenomena such as the illusory truth effect and the sharing of information known to be false.
title: Behavioral Outcomes of Human Cognitive Security within an ... - arXiv

note: A Tradecraft Primer: Structured Analytic Techniques for Improving. Intelligence Analysis. https://ww...
title: [PDF] Critical Thinking techniques - CDSE

note: This is a peer-reviewed article in Connections: The Quarterly Journal (Vol. 21, No. 2, Spring 2022) by Peter Dobias and Kyle Christensen examining military competition below the threshold of armed conflict. It analyzes how Russia, China, and Iran operate in the space between peace and war, describing Russia's emphasis on information measures, China's maritime and force approaches, and Iran's anti-access and proxy strategies. The authors argue NATO lacks adequate sub-threshold deterrence and propose developing intermediate, non-lethal force capabilities.
title: The 'Grey Zone' and Hybrid Activities | connections-qj.org

note: An article by Dr. Robert S. Burrell on the Irregular Warfare Initiative arguing that U.S. military doctrine inadequately addresses gray zone and hybrid warfare. It proposes a two-dimensional framework spanning peace-to-war and direct-to-indirect means, yielding four approaches: traditional warfare, deterrence, irregular warfare, and competition. The piece uses examples such as Russian and Chinese activities to recommend doctrine that better anticipates and counters ambiguous, below-threshold conflict.
title: A Full Spectrum of Conflict Design: How Doctrine Should Embrace ...

note: An article in the U.S. Army's Military Intelligence Professional Bulletin on countering gray zone threats. It discusses how adversaries operate below the threshold of open conflict using hybrid activities across tactical, operational, and strategic levels, and considers how military intelligence can identify and attribute actors who attempt to remain anonymous. The piece is framed around adapting intelligence practice to contemporary competition short of war.
title: Anonymous No More: Countering the Gray Zone Threat - from MIPB

note: This U.S. Army Training Circular (TC 18-01) defines the Special Forces concept for planning and conducting unconventional warfare operations, covering how units enable and support resistance movements in denied or hostile territory. It presents a seven-phase operational framework encompassing psychological preparation, infiltration, organization of underground and guerrilla forces, employment, and transition. The doctrine addresses feasibility assessment based on terrain, government strength, population will, and resistance movement cohesion, as well as integration with information support and civil affairs functions. The manual is intended as a non-prescriptive guide for understanding how unconventional warfare fits within broader irregular warfare strategy at team through battalion levels.
title: Full text of "Special Forces Unconventional Warfare"

note: U.S. Army Field Manual FM 3-05.130, "Army Special Operations Forces Unconventional Warfare," published by Headquarters, Department of the Army in September 2008. The manual is organized into chapters covering an introduction to warfare types, the instruments of United States national power, national policy and doctrine, and planning considerations including the seven phases of unconventional warfare and the role of Army Special Operations Forces. It addresses interagency and supporting activities and is marked with U.S. Government distribution restrictions.
title: [PDF] FM 3-05.130 Army Special Operations Forces Unconventional ...

note: FM 3-05.130, Army Special Operations Forces Unconventional Warfare (Headquarters, Department of the Army, 30 September 2008). The manual defines unconventional warfare and situates it within U.S. national power, covering the seven phases of UW operations, Special Forces missions, psychological operations, civil affairs operations in the UW context, and the roles of supporting elements including interagency partners. Distribution was restricted to U.S. Government agencies and contractors.
title: Army Special Operations Forces Unconventional Warfare

note: FM 3-05.102, Army Special Operations Forces Intelligence (Headquarters, Department of the Army). The manual addresses intelligence and electronic warfare (IEW) support across Army Special Operations Force components, covering Special Forces, Rangers, ARSOA, PSYOP, and Civil Affairs operations. It explains ARSOF IEW characteristics, the intelligence cycle as applied to special operations, intelligence disciplines, and the systems and architecture supporting multinational and interagency intelligence sharing.
title: Army Special Operations Forces Intelligence

note: A U.S. Army field manual, FM 3-05.102 (Army Special Operations Forces Intelligence), dated 2001 and superseding FM 34-36, distributed with restrictions to U.S. government agencies. The manual gives special operations forces commanders and staff a doctrinal overview of intelligence support to their missions, covering force structure, training, and operational requirements consistent with joint doctrine. Its appendices detail mission planning folder formats and target intelligence packages for special operations tasks across the range of military operations.
title: [PDF] Army Special Operations Forces Intelligence

note: An article from Small Wars Journal (affiliated with Arizona State University) announcing that The Resistance Hub has compiled declassified Office of Strategic Services (OSS) manuals for public access. The OSS was the WWII predecessor to the CIA, and the documents illustrate how the United States organized clandestine activities in occupied Europe and Asia. The article presents these materials for historical study of the origins of contemporary irregular warfare concepts and doctrine.
title: Office of Strategic Services (OSS) Manuals | The Resistance Hub

note: A digitized World War II field manual from the Internet Archive, the Special Operations Field Manual, Strategic Services (provisional), issued July 18, 1944 by the U.S. Office of Strategic Services (OSS). The 32-page manual sets out the authorized functions, operational plans, methods, and organization of OSS Maritime Units and the broader Special Operations Branch. Classified as an obsolete military manual now in the public domain, it documents American special operations and intelligence doctrine of the period and is available in multiple digital formats.
title: Special Operations Field Manual, Strategic Services (provisional ...

note: This commemorative book begins with a primer on OSS wartime activities and functions and republished...
title: [PDF] office of strategic services - ARSOF History

note: Principles of Tradecraft, a 1995 publication by Militia Free Press (a subsidiary of The Resister). The document presents an overview of intelligence and espionage concepts, with chapters covering an introduction to espionage, agents (typology, identification, recruitment, and handling), and agent organization and management including personnel and structures. It is framed as an introductory treatment rather than a comprehensive reference on intelligence requirements.
title: [PDF] PRINCIPLES TRADECRAFT

note: This is a Blockint blog post by Ludo Block, published in October 2022, analyzing a KGB training manual on information handling released by the Free Russia Foundation. The manual, written for foreign intelligence officers, sets out six requirements for valuable intelligence and a framework for evaluating sources based on past performance and competence. The author argues that despite its Cold War origins the manual likely still informs current Russian intelligence practice, and uses it for comparative analysis of information-evaluation systems.
title: How the KGB handled information - Blockint

note: A Free Russia Foundation Think Tank project, "The Lubyanka Files," documenting the history of Soviet and Russian security services through declassified records and expert analysis. It features work by Michael Weiss examining a collection of KGB training manuals from the 1960s through the 1980s, including recruitment practices and the handling of foreign nationals as confidential contacts. The project aims to inform understanding of repression and surveillance and to support pro-democracy analysis of Russia.
title: The Lubyanka Files - Free Russia Foundation THINK TANK

note: A 1993 Paladin Press publication presenting a translated Soviet special-operations training text attributed to A. I. Dolmatov, accompanied by an extensive preface by Western trainer Jim Shortt. The preface traces the evolution of Soviet special forces from the 1917 revolution through the Cold War and describes organizations such as the KGB, MVD, and GRU and units including Alpha, Spetsnaz, and OMON. The publisher presents the material strictly for historical and reference purposes.
title: Full text of "KGB Alpha Team Training Manual" - Internet Archive

note: This article draws on the Mitrokhin Archive, compiled by defected KGB archivist Vasili Mitrokhin, to document Soviet state surveillance of dissident philosopher Alexander Zinoviev, who was assigned the codename Logician by the KGB's Fifth Chief Directorate. Zinoviev faced professional retaliation including revocation of his doctorate and expulsion from the Communist Party following the publication of satirical anti-Soviet works such as Yawning Heights. After emigrating to Bonn in 1978, the KGB residency there received orders to continue monitoring his activities abroad. The article illustrates how Soviet intelligence services used persistent surveillance and professional harassment to neutralize intellectual dissent.
title: The Logician (The Zinoviev's case). Folder 46. The Chekist ...

note: The Wilson Center Digital Archive link for the Mitrokhin Archive is a dead staging domain. The Mitrokhin Archive documents Soviet KGB history; use the published Cambridge University Press edition or Wilson Center production archive instead.
title: Mitrokhin Archive

note: A Wikipedia article on the Mitrokhin Archive, a collection of handwritten notes made by KGB archivist Vasili Mitrokhin documenting Soviet intelligence operations from the 1930s through the 1980s. Mitrokhin secretly copied files between 1972 and 1984 while overseeing the relocation of KGB foreign intelligence archives, and after the Soviet collapse defected to the UK in 1992 with the material. The notes describe recruitment of agents, disinformation campaigns, and other operations, and formed the basis for Christopher Andrew's books; scholars note the absence of original documents limits independent verification.
title: Mitrokhin Archive - Wikipedia

note: An Internet Archive record for The Mitrokhin Archive: The KGB and the World, a two-volume work by Christopher Andrew published in 1999 by Allen Lane, running to roughly 1,050 pages. The volumes draw on materials compiled by former KGB officer Vasili Mitrokhin to document Soviet intelligence operations, with the first volume covering the KGB in Europe and the West and the second addressing the KGB and the wider world. It examines the organization's espionage networks and foreign operations during the Cold War, with bibliographical references and indexing for historical research.
title: The Mitrokhin archive : the KGB and the world

note: An Internet Archive full-text scan of the proceedings of a 1991 National Archives conference on the Office of Strategic Services (OSS), the U.S. wartime intelligence organization, published as "The Secrets War: The Office of Strategic Services in World War II." The volume collects scholarly papers on the agency's founding under the Coordinator of Information, its recruitment of talent from elite universities and specialists, and the tension between intelligence analysis and disinformation work. It also notes the research value of the declassified OSS records held in Record Group 226.
title: Full text of "The Secret War The Office of Strategic Services in World ...

note: A resource directory page from the Office of Strategic Services Society, a historical organization dedicated to preserving the record of the WWII-era OSS. The page functions as a curated library of links to external sites covering OSS history, including operations, related organizations, notable figures such as William Donovan and Allen Dulles, educational materials, and geographic theaters. It serves researchers, veterans, and history enthusiasts seeking dispersed OSS-related archives and museum materials.
title: OSS Resources - Office of Strategic Services Society

note: A reproduction of a Cold War-era manual written by magician John Mulholland for the CIA, presented with commentary by H. Keith Melton and Robert Wallace as "The Official CIA Manual of Trickery and Deception." The book situates the manual within the history of the MKULTRA program and the long-missing magic manuals, and reproduces Mulholland's guidance on applying sleight-of-hand and misdirection techniques to covert operational tasks. It includes a foreword by John McLaughlin and an introduction explaining the document's provenance and significance to intelligence history.
title: [PDF] The Official CIA Manual of Trickery and Deception

note: The Center for the Study of Intelligence (CSI) serves as a producer and repository of unclassified i...
title: Center for the Study of Intelligence - CSI - CIA

note: Studies in Intelligence is the CIA's professional journal, published by the Center for the Study of Intelligence (CSI). It covers historical, analytical, and methodological topics across the intelligence profession, and is issued quarterly in partially declassified extract form. As of 2026 the archive holds 110 issues, with the most recent being Volume 70, No. 1 (March 2026); articles are written by intelligence community professionals under their own names or pen names.
title: Studies in Intelligence

note: This is a National Security Archive briefing book presenting declassified articles from the CIA's internal journal Studies in Intelligence, first published in 1955. The collection gathers articles obtained through Freedom of Information Act requests and litigation, spanning CIA history from the Kennedy era through the post-9/11 period. Topics include intelligence analysis, institutional history, legal issues, and interviews with intelligence leaders, and the briefing highlights differences between voluntary and litigation-compelled disclosure.
title: Studies in Intelligence: New Articles from The CIA's In-House Journal

note: How To Be A Spy: The WW2 SOE Training Manual - Denis Rigden · The CIA Guide to Clandestine Operation...
title: What is your reading list for (counter)intelligence/tradecraft? - Reddit

note: A briefing from Unredacted, a UK investigative outlet, covering the early development of the UKUSA Agreement, the postwar US-UK signals-intelligence framework that became the basis for the Five Eyes alliance. It traces the 1946 BRUSA agreement on intelligence exchange, Canada's negotiation toward equal partnership in 1949, and the gradual integration of Australia and New Zealand through the 1950s. It notes that by the 1960s agencies relied on trust and case-by-case arrangements rather than formal updates to the written agreement.
title: The origins of the UKUSA Agreement - Unredacted

note: The Mossad is concerned with foreign intelligence gathering, intelligence analysis, and covert opera...
title: Mossad's secretive operations and reputation - Facebook

note: An arXiv paper surveying the shift from passive language models to Agentic AI, where large language models act as cognitive controllers that combine memory, tool use, and environmental feedback to pursue extended goals. It proposes a unified taxonomy organizing agent systems into six components: perception, brain, planning, action, tool use, and collaboration. The paper also reviews open standards for tool integration and discusses challenges such as hallucination during action, infinite loops, and prompt injection.
title: [2601.12560] Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI) - arXiv

note: An arXiv survey paper on agentic AI examining the shift from generative systems to autonomous agents that perceive, reason, plan, and act. It proposes a unified taxonomy decomposing LLM-based agents into six dimensions: core components, cognitive architecture, learning paradigms, multi-agent systems, environments and domains, and evaluation and safety. The paper emphasizes engineering and deployment concerns, linking failure modes such as hallucination, infinite loops, and prompt injection to architectural choices, and discusses standardized connectors and orchestration controllers.
title: Agentic Artificial Intelligence (AI): Architectures, Taxonomies, and ...

note: A LinkedIn post by Aishwarya Srinivasan discussing agentic AI design patterns for building production-grade AI agents. It describes four capabilities beyond simple prompt-to-output systems: reflection, in which agents self-evaluate after each action; tool use, in which agents call code, data, and APIs; planning, in which agents decompose goals into sequenced steps and update plans as conditions change; and multi-agent collaboration, in which specialized agents coordinate through shared memory and communication. The post argues effective agent design requires layered systems with runtime feedback rather than monolithic model chains.
title: Agentic AI Design Patterns by Andrew Ng - LinkedIn

note: An OECD Artificial Intelligence Paper (No. 56, February 2026) titled "The Agentic AI Landscape and Its Conceptual Foundations," prepared by OECD staff with an expert group. It analyzes how the terms "AI agents" and "agentic AI" are defined across the literature and relates their key features to the OECD AI system definition, finding that agentic AI emphasizes coordination among multiple agents, task decomposition and delegation, and sustained operation in less predictable environments with limited human oversight. The report also presents descriptive evidence on adoption trends and notes that further progress is needed toward more trustworthy systems.
title: [PDF] The agentic AI landscape and its conceptual foundations | OECD

note: An MIT Sloan explainer article on agentic AI, defining autonomous software systems that perceive, reason, and act to complete multi-step tasks with minimal human intervention. It distinguishes such agents from chatbots, cites a 2025 MIT Sloan/BCG survey finding 35% of companies had deployed agents with 44% planning to, and reviews business applications such as fraud detection and customer service. It also stresses that most implementation effort goes to data engineering and governance, and outlines risks around reliability, security, and accountability.
title: Agentic AI, explained | MIT Sloan

note: The arXiv abstract page for "Agentic AI: A Comprehensive Survey of Architectures, Applications," a 2025 paper by Mohamad Abou Ali and Fadi Dornaika. Based on a PRISMA systematic review of roughly 90 studies from 2018 to 2025, it proposes a dual-paradigm framework distinguishing symbolic/classical systems based on algorithmic planning and persistent state from neural/generative systems driven by stochastic generation and prompt orchestration. The survey examines theoretical foundations, domain applications in healthcare, finance, and robotics, and governance considerations, concluding that paradigm choice is strategic and advocating hybrid neuro-symbolic approaches.
title: Agentic AI: A Comprehensive Survey of Architectures, Applications ...

note: A Medium article by Anil Jain (2025) surveying agentic AI system architectures and design patterns. It defines agentic AI as systems capable of independent decision-making, reviews frameworks such as LangChain, LlamaIndex, and AutoGen, and describes five system components: perception, decision-making, action, memory, and communication. It compares single-agent and multi-agent designs and details core patterns including reflection, tool use, ReAct, planning, and multi-agent collaboration, along with subtypes such as sequential, hierarchical, and router arrangements.
title: Agentic AI Architectures And Design Patterns | by Anil Jain

note: An AWS Prescriptive Guidance document (July 2025) introducing agentic AI patterns and workflows. It presents reusable design templates for building AI agent systems that operate with autonomy while remaining controllable and aligned with goals, aimed at architects, developers, and product leaders. The guide covers agent patterns (reasoning, retrieval-augmented, coding, voice, and multi-agent designs), how agents use large language models for reasoning, and workflow patterns for task orchestration, coordination, observability, and control mapped to cloud-native architectures.
title: Agentic AI patterns and workflows on AWS

note: This is an arXiv research paper by Sivasathivel Kandasamy titled "Control Plane as a Tool: A Scalable Design Pattern for Agentic AI Systems." It proposes an architectural pattern in which AI agents interact with a single control-plane interface that handles tool routing and selection, rather than exposing many tools directly. The paper argues this decouples tool management from agent reasoning and improves modularity, governance, and auditability, comparing the approach to existing frameworks for tool orchestration.
title: A Scalable Design Pattern for Agentic AI Systems

note: An Exabeam explainer describing agentic AI frameworks as software toolkits that provide pre-built components and architectures for building autonomous AI agents. It outlines core elements such as agent coordination, tool integration, memory management, workflow definition, and deployment monitoring, and reviews eight frameworks including LangGraph, AutoGen, CrewAI, LlamaIndex, Haystack, DSPy, and Semantic Kernel. It closes with implementation best practices emphasizing small prototypes, human oversight, and standardized interfaces.
title: Agentic AI Frameworks: Key Components & Top 8 Options in 2026

note: Security analysis of major AI agent frameworks including LangChain, CrewAI, AutoGen, Semantic Kernel...
title: Agent Framework Security | redteams.ai

note: This article by Riverside Research traces AI's evolution from large language models through retrieval-augmented systems, single-task agents, collaborative agentic systems, and toward emergent multi-agent autonomy capable of managing surveillance and decision support at operational speed. Author Matthew May describes each stage in terms of increasing autonomy and mission scope, including systems that can independently monitor feeds, generate reports, and decompose high-level objectives into coordinated subtasks. The article emphasizes that human operators remain essential throughout, setting objectives and retaining final decision authority while autonomous systems improve speed and situational awareness. The piece argues that national security organizations must proactively adopt these capabilities to maintain strategic advantage.
title: How Riverside Research Advances Agentic AI for National Security

note: A 2025 blog article from Simbian AI explaining the application of agentic AI to security operations centers. It describes an architecture organized into perception, cognitive, and action modules, and a coordinated framework of specialized agents for tasks such as alert triage, threat investigation, and vulnerability management. The piece also examines implementation barriers including computational demands, multi-agent security weaknesses, and data quality, concluding that effective deployments keep humans in the loop for complex decisions.
title: What is Agentic AI in Security Operations? Autonomous SOC Agents ...

note: An article from Cybersecurity Tribe examining where autonomous AI should and should not operate within security operations, drawing on survey data and commentary from numerous security experts. It identifies high-volume, repetitive tasks such as alert triage and policy validation as well suited to automation, while warning against autonomous handling of irreversible or high-impact decisions affecting infrastructure and access. The central recommendation is graduated autonomy that augments rather than replaces human judgment, with strong governance, escalation paths, and comprehensive logging to preserve accountability.
title: The Right Role for Agentic AI in Security Operations
